question: Why does a Spring Boot app consume more memory over time?
answer: Memory growth over time in Spring Boot apps is almost always a leak, not a bug in Spring itself. The most common causes are: (1) Unbounded caches — using @Cacheable with no eviction policy means the cache grows forever. Always configure a maximum size and TTL. (2) ThreadLocal leaks — if ThreadLocals are set in request threads managed by Tomcat but never removed, the value stays referenced by the thread even after the request ends. In thread pools threads are reused, so the old value is visible to the next request. Always call remove() in a finally block. (3) Static collections — a static Map or List that accumulates entries per request but is never cleared. (4) Listeners and event handlers registered but never deregistered. (5) Hibernate first-level cache holding large object graphs when sessions are kept open too long. (6) MetaSpace growth due to dynamic class generation (CGLIB proxies, reflection, Groovy scripts). To diagnose: enable GC logging, take heap dumps at intervals, use jmap -histo to see which classes are growing. Tools like Eclipse MAT or VisualVM can identify the retention path — the chain of references keeping objects alive. In production, configure JVM flags: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp so you capture the state at the moment of failure.

question: How do you detect bean initialization issues in large applications?
answer: In large Spring Boot applications with hundreds of beans, initialization failures can be cryptic. Several strategies help. First, enable debug logging for the Spring container: set logging.level.org.springframework=DEBUG in application.properties. This prints every bean being instantiated and wired. Second, use the spring.main.lazy-initialization=true flag — this defers bean creation to first use, so startup succeeds even if some beans have issues, and you get a clear stack trace pointing to the failing bean when it is first accessed. Third, implement ApplicationListener<ApplicationFailedEvent> to capture and log the full cause chain when startup fails. Fourth, use Spring Boot Actuator's /actuator/beans endpoint (when the app does start) to list all registered beans and spot unexpected duplicates or missing entries. Fifth, for conditional beans, add logging inside @Conditional implementations to trace why a bean was or was not created. Sixth, annotate critical initialization with @PostConstruct and wrap in try-catch with detailed logging so the first failure is visible before Spring propagates it. In multi-module projects, a common trap is a bean defined in a library module whose package is not covered by @ComponentScan in the main application — the bean simply does not exist and injection fails with NoSuchBeanDefinitionException. Always verify package boundaries.

question: What happens if @PostConstruct throws an exception?
answer: @PostConstruct is called by Spring after dependency injection is complete but before the bean is made available to the rest of the application. If a @PostConstruct method throws any exception (checked or unchecked), Spring wraps it in a BeanCreationException and the entire application context fails to start. This is by design — Spring treats initialization as a critical phase. The stack trace will show 'Error creating bean with name X: Invocation of init method failed'. There is no automatic retry; the application shuts down. Key implications: (1) Any code in @PostConstruct that calls a database, external service, or file system can cause startup failure if those resources are unavailable. This is a common cause of container restart loops in Kubernetes. (2) If the bean is a singleton (default), that one failure prevents all other beans that depend on it from being created. (3) For beans with @Scope("prototype"), the exception occurs each time a new instance is requested. Mitigation strategies: wrap initialization logic in try-catch inside @PostConstruct and log instead of throw for non-critical setup; use @Retryable (Spring Retry) on the method if the resource might be temporarily unavailable; consider moving heavy initialization to an ApplicationRunner or CommandLineRunner which runs after the context is fully started.

question: Why does @Value sometimes fail to inject properties?
answer: @Value injection failures are one of the most common Spring Boot gotchas. The root causes: (1) The property key is misspelled or does not exist in any loaded property source — Spring injects the literal placeholder string '${my.prop}' if no default is given, or throws IllegalArgumentException. Always provide a default: @Value("${my.prop:defaultValue}"). (2) The bean using @Value is instantiated too early in the container lifecycle, before PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer has been registered. This happens most often in @Configuration classes that also contain @Bean methods — the class itself is instantiated before all post-processors are ready. Solution: inject via constructor or use Environment.getProperty(). (3) @Value does not work in classes that are not Spring-managed (created with new instead of injected). (4) In @Configuration classes annotated with @Bean, if the class is not proxied (e.g., uses proxyBeanMethods=false) and you call another @Bean method directly, @Value in that bean may not be processed. (5) Profile-specific property files are not loaded because the active profile is not set. (6) Encoding issues with special characters in property files — use UTF-8 and escape colons and backslashes.

question: How does Spring Boot decide the order of auto-configurations?
answer: Spring Boot's auto-configuration ordering is controlled through several mechanisms. At the core, Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Boot 2.7+) or META-INF/spring.factories to discover all auto-configuration classes. Order is then determined by: (1) @AutoConfigureBefore and @AutoConfigureAfter annotations — these declare explicit ordering dependencies between auto-configurations. For example, DataSourceAutoConfiguration must run before HibernateJpaAutoConfiguration. (2) @AutoConfigureOrder with a numeric value, lower values run first. (3) @Conditional annotations filter which configurations actually activate. @ConditionalOnClass checks classpath, @ConditionalOnMissingBean prevents overriding user-defined beans, @ConditionalOnProperty checks property values. The ordering matters because one auto-configuration may depend on beans created by another. In practice, you rarely need to care about this unless writing custom starters. To debug ordering: set logging.level.org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure=DEBUG or run with --debug flag. The 'CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT' section shows every auto-configuration, whether it matched, and why. To exclude a specific auto-configuration: @SpringBootApplication(exclude = {DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class}) or set spring.autoconfigure.exclude property.

question: What are the risks of enabling too many Actuator endpoints?
answer: Spring Boot Actuator endpoints expose internal application state and can be a significant security risk if not properly controlled. Key risks: (1) /actuator/env exposes all environment variables and properties — including database passwords, API keys, and secrets. Even with partial masking, sensitive keys can be inferred. (2) /actuator/heapdump allows downloading a full JVM heap dump which can contain user PII, session tokens, and credentials in memory. (3) /actuator/loggers allows changing log levels at runtime — an attacker could enable TRACE logging to flood disk and cause a DoS. (4) /actuator/shutdown (if enabled) terminates the JVM. (5) /actuator/beans and /actuator/mappings reveal internal architecture, making targeted attacks easier. (6) /actuator/metrics exposes request counts and timings that can reveal business logic. Best practices: expose only health and info endpoints publicly; require authentication for all others; never expose actuator on the public-facing port — use a separate management port (management.server.port=8081) and restrict that port to internal networks only; use Spring Security to lock down all /actuator/** paths.
codeExample:
# application.properties — secure configuration
# Only expose health and info publicly
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info
# Never in prod: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

# Use separate management port (internal network only)
management.server.port=8081
management.endpoint.health.show-details=when_authorized

# Spring Security — lock down actuator
@Configuration
public class ActuatorSecurity extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {
    @Override
    protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http.requestMatcher(
                EndpointRequest.toAnyEndpoint())
            .authorizeRequests()
            .requestMatchers(
                EndpointRequest.to(
                    HealthEndpoint.class,
                    InfoEndpoint.class))
            .permitAll()
            .anyRequest().hasRole("ACTUATOR_ADMIN")
            .and().httpBasic();
    }
}

# Mask sensitive values in /env
management.endpoint.env.keys-to-sanitize=\
  password,secret,key,token,credentials,vcap_services

question: Why does your app behave differently after scaling pods?
answer: When a Spring Boot application is scaled to multiple pods (instances), several categories of issues emerge. (1) In-memory state is not shared — any state stored in JVM memory (static variables, local caches, session state, in-memory queues) is per-pod. A request routed to Pod A will not see data written by Pod B. Fix: externalize state to Redis, a database, or a message broker. (2) Scheduled tasks (@Scheduled) run in every pod — if a job runs every 5 minutes and you have 10 pods, it runs 10 times. Fix: use ShedLock or Quartz clustered scheduler to ensure single execution. (3) File system access — pods typically have ephemeral storage. A file written in one pod is invisible to others. Fix: use shared storage (S3, NFS) or a database. (4) Health check warm-up — a new pod may receive traffic before it has fully warmed its caches or established DB connections. Use readiness probes distinct from liveness probes. (5) Session affinity — if sessions are stored in-memory and the load balancer routes the same user to different pods, they lose their session. Fix: use Redis-backed sessions with Spring Session. (6) Configuration drift — if a pod was restarted with a different environment variable set, it runs with different config. Use Spring Cloud Config Server for centralized config.

question: How does Spring Boot handle classpath scanning internally?
answer: Spring Boot classpath scanning is triggered by @ComponentScan, which is included in @SpringBootApplication via @EnableAutoConfiguration and @SpringBootConfiguration. Internally: (1) ClassPathBeanDefinitionScanner is instantiated. It uses ASM (not reflection) to read class bytecode, so it does NOT load every class — it only reads metadata. This makes scanning fast even for large classpaths. (2) It looks for classes with specific annotations: @Component (and meta-annotations like @Service, @Repository, @Controller). It also picks up JSR-330 @Named. (3) The scanner reads a file index if available (META-INF/spring.components, generated by spring-context-indexer) — this completely bypasses filesystem scanning and is a significant startup optimization for large applications. (4) Filter rules are applied: includeFilters and excludeFilters on @ComponentScan control which classes are considered. (5) For each qualifying class, a BeanDefinition is created and registered in the BeanDefinitionRegistry. Actual instantiation happens later. Common problems: if the main class is not in the root package, sub-packages are missed. If you have components in third-party jars not under the scanned package, they are missed unless you add their package to @ComponentScan.

question: What causes duplicate bean registration in multi-module projects?
answer: Duplicate bean registration is a frequent issue in multi-module Maven/Gradle projects. Root causes: (1) The same @Component class is on the classpath twice — e.g., a common module is included as a dependency AND its classes are also compiled directly into the main module. Maven shade plugin or fat-jar packaging can cause this. (2) @ComponentScan in multiple @Configuration classes overlap — two different config classes scan the same package, registering beans twice. (3) Auto-configuration registers a bean and the user code also defines one of the same type and name. Spring's behavior depends on spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding (default false in Boot 2.1+) — if false, it throws a BeanDefinitionOverrideException at startup. (4) A library jar includes its own @SpringBootApplication which triggers its own component scan when your application starts. Fix: libraries should NOT use @SpringBootApplication; use @Configuration with explicit @Import instead. Diagnosis: enable debug logging and look for 'Overriding bean definition for bean' messages or check the startup exception message which names the conflicting bean and both source locations.

question: Why does your API return correct data but response time fluctuates?
answer: Inconsistent response times with correct results typically point to external resource contention, not logic bugs. Investigation areas: (1) Database connection pool saturation — when all HikariCP connections are in use, new requests wait. Check hikari.maximum-pool-size and monitor pool metrics via /actuator/metrics/hikari.connections.pending. (2) GC pauses — Stop-The-World GC events pause all threads for tens to hundreds of milliseconds. Enable GC logging: -Xlog:gc* and correlate pause times with slow requests. (3) External service latency — calls to downstream services have variable response times. Use timeouts and bulkhead patterns to prevent one slow downstream from affecting all threads. (4) Thread pool queue buildup — Tomcat's thread pool (default 200 threads) can exhaust under high load. Long requests hold threads causing queuing for new requests. (5) JIT compilation warming — the first few hundred requests are interpreted; JIT-compiled code is much faster. Response times normalize after warmup. (6) Network-level jitter — TCP retransmits, DNS lookup caches expiring. (7) Logging I/O — synchronous logging to disk on every request. Switch to async logging: use AsyncAppender in Logback.

question: How do you control thread usage in Spring Boot applications?
answer: Spring Boot uses threads in several places: Tomcat's request thread pool, @Async thread pools, @Scheduled executors, and Spring WebFlux worker threads. Controlling each: (1) Tomcat threads: server.tomcat.threads.max (default 200) and server.tomcat.threads.min-spare (default 10). Size based on expected concurrency and request duration. (2) @Async threads: if you call @EnableAsync without a custom Executor, Spring uses SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor which creates a new thread per task — a thread bomb. Always define a ThreadPoolTaskExecutor bean. (3) @Scheduled: default is a single-threaded ThreadPoolTaskScheduler. If one task is slow it blocks all scheduled tasks. Configure taskScheduler bean with pool size. (4) Virtual threads (Java 21+): Spring Boot 3.2+ supports Project Loom virtual threads. Set spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true to use virtual threads for Tomcat and @Async, eliminating the need to manually size most thread pools. Monitoring: use /actuator/metrics/tomcat.threads.busy and /actuator/metrics/executor.active to track usage in real time.

question: What happens when application.yml and application.properties both exist?
answer: Spring Boot loads both files but applies a specific precedence rule: application.properties takes higher priority over application.yml when both are present. Both files are read and merged, but if the same key is defined in both, the value from application.properties wins. The precedence order (highest to lowest) across all sources is: command-line arguments, Java system properties (-D flags), OS environment variables, profile-specific files (application-{profile}.properties or .yml), application.properties, application.yml, @PropertySource annotations, and finally default properties. Within the same file format, a profile-specific file (application-prod.properties) overrides the base file (application.properties). In practice, mixing both formats in a project causes confusion. Best practice: pick one format and stick to it throughout the project. If you must use both (e.g., base config in yml, secrets in properties), document the precedence clearly. Note: Spring Boot 2.4+ changed how multi-document YAML files and profile-specific config is loaded — activating profiles via spring.profiles.active inside YAML using --- document separators behaves differently from pre-2.4.
codeExample:
# application.yml
server:
  port: 8080
app:
  name: MyService
  timeout: 30

# application.properties
# This takes HIGHER precedence
app.timeout=60  # This wins — overrides yml value of 30

# Verify which value is active:
# GET /actuator/env/app.timeout
# Shows: value=60, source=application.properties

# Spring Boot property source precedence (simplified)
# 1. Command line: --app.timeout=90
# 2. System property: -Dapp.timeout=90
# 3. OS env: APP_TIMEOUT=90
# 4. application-{profile}.properties
# 5. application.properties   <-- higher
# 6. application.yml          <-- lower

# Multi-document YAML (Boot 2.4+)
# application.yml
app:
  name: base
---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
app:
  name: production

question: Why do custom exception handlers sometimes not trigger?
answer: Custom exception handlers defined in @ControllerAdvice or @RestControllerAdvice can silently fail to trigger for several reasons. (1) Exception is thrown outside the controller layer — if an exception occurs in a Filter or Interceptor before the request reaches a controller, @ControllerAdvice does not handle it. Spring's DispatcherServlet only routes exceptions from controllers to @ControllerAdvice. Exceptions in filters need a custom filter-level handler or ErrorController implementation. (2) Exception is not the exact type matched — if you handle RuntimeException but throw a subclass not covered by the handler, Spring may use a more specific handler from another @ControllerAdvice first. Use @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class) as a catch-all. (3) Multiple @ControllerAdvice beans — Spring evaluates them in order. If one handles an exception and doesn't re-throw, others are skipped. (4) ResponseBody annotation missing — in a non-@RestControllerAdvice, you must add @ResponseBody to @ExceptionHandler methods or return ResponseEntity. (5) Spring Security exceptions (AuthenticationException, AccessDeniedException) are handled by Spring Security's filter chain, not @ControllerAdvice. You must configure AuthenticationEntryPoint and AccessDeniedHandler in the security configuration.

question: How do you handle large payloads without killing performance?
answer: Large payload handling requires streaming instead of buffering. By default, Spring Boot reads the entire request body into memory before processing, and serializes the entire response into memory before sending — disastrous for multi-MB payloads under concurrent load. Solutions: (1) Streaming request parsing — for file uploads use MultipartFile with streaming: instead of byte[] use InputStream. Configure spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size and max-request-size. (2) Streaming response output — use StreamingResponseBody to write to the response OutputStream directly without buffering the entire response. (3) For JSON: Jackson can be configured to stream large arrays using JsonGenerator. (4) Pagination — never return unbounded lists; always add page and size parameters. (5) Compression — enable HTTP compression: server.compression.enabled=true. Responses over 2KB are gzip-compressed, reducing bandwidth by 60-80%. (6) Virtual threads or reactive — with many concurrent large payload requests, Tomcat threads block waiting for I/O. WebFlux with reactive streams handles backpressure natively. (7) Set Tomcat max-swallow-size to limit how much of an aborted request body Tomcat reads.

question: Why does Hibernate generate unexpected queries?
answer: Unexpected Hibernate queries are one of the most common performance issues in Spring Boot JPA applications. The primary causes: (1) N+1 problem — a query for N parent entities results in N additional queries for each child. Caused by LAZY associations being accessed outside the original query. Fix: use JOIN FETCH, @EntityGraph, or @BatchSize. (2) Eager fetching by default — @OneToOne and @ManyToOne are EAGER by default. Even if you don't need the associated entity, Hibernate fetches it. Fix: explicitly set fetch = FetchType.LAZY on all associations. (3) Dirty checking — Hibernate's first-level cache tracks all loaded entities. On transaction commit, it compares entity state to its snapshot and issues UPDATE statements for changed fields, even if you didn't intend to update. Fix: use @Transactional(readOnly=true) for read operations. (4) Collection initialization — accessing a lazy collection outside a transaction triggers LazyInitializationException, or if inside a transaction, triggers a separate query per entity. (5) Implicit joins — JPQL queries with unqualified path navigation generate implicit inner joins. To diagnose: set spring.jpa.show-sql=true and hibernate.format_sql=true, or use p6spy for production query logging.

question: How do you debug a deadlock in a Spring Boot service?
answer: Deadlocks in Spring Boot services can occur at two levels: JVM thread deadlocks and database-level deadlocks. JVM deadlocks: two threads hold locks and each waits for the other's lock. Detection: use jstack <pid> or jcmd <pid> Thread.print. The JVM will print 'Found 1 deadlock' with the thread names, locks held, and locks waiting. Java's ThreadMXBean can detect deadlocks programmatically. Database deadlocks: two transactions each hold a row lock the other needs. PostgreSQL and MySQL detect and abort one transaction with a deadlock error (error code 40P01 / ER_LOCK_DEADLOCK). Spring's @Transactional re-throws this as CannotAcquireLockException. Fix database deadlocks by: (1) always accessing tables in the same order across transactions, (2) using SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for queue-like patterns, (3) keeping transactions short, (4) using optimistic locking for low-contention scenarios. For JVM deadlocks: (1) avoid nested synchronized blocks with different lock orders, (2) use tryLock() with timeout instead of lock(), (3) use higher-level concurrency utilities that avoid manual locking.

question: What happens if a BeanFactoryPostProcessor fails?
answer: BeanFactoryPostProcessors (BFPP) are one of the earliest extension points in the Spring container lifecycle. They run after bean definitions are loaded but before bean instantiation begins. If a BFPP throws an exception: (1) The exception propagates directly and the ApplicationContext refresh fails completely — no beans are created. (2) Unlike @PostConstruct failures (which fail one bean at a time), a BFPP failure kills the entire context initialization. (3) The error is wrapped in ApplicationContextException with the message 'Unable to start'. (4) Other registered BFPPs may or may not have run depending on ordering. Well-known BFPPs include PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer (resolves @Value placeholders) and ConfigurationClassPostProcessor (processes @Configuration classes). If PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer fails — e.g., because a required property file is missing — ALL @Value injections fail. Best practices: BFPPs should not perform I/O or external calls. They should only modify bean definitions. If you need to validate config at startup, prefer ApplicationRunner or @PostConstruct on a specific validation bean so the failure is scoped and has a clear name in the stack trace.

question: How do you avoid startup failure due to missing configs?
answer: Startup failures due to missing configuration are common in environments where secrets are injected at runtime (Kubernetes secrets, Vault, AWS Parameter Store). Strategies to avoid them: (1) Provide defaults for non-critical properties using @Value("${prop:default}") or ConfigurationProperties with default field values. (2) Use @ConditionalOnProperty to skip auto-configuration that requires an external service: if the property is absent, the feature is disabled rather than failing. (3) Use spring-cloud-starter-config with spring.cloud.config.fail-fast=false so a missing Config Server does not crash the app — it falls back to local config. (4) Validate configuration at startup (not during bean initialization) using @Validated on @ConfigurationProperties beans — this gives clear error messages listing all invalid fields. (5) In Kubernetes, use init containers to verify secrets are mounted before the app container starts. (6) For Spring Boot 3.x, use @ImportRuntimeHints to declare config requirements that can be validated during native image compilation. (7) Use Jasypt or Spring Cloud Vault for encrypted properties that are decrypted at startup — if the encryption key is missing the error is explicit.

question: Why does Spring Boot retry DB connections on startup?
answer: Spring Boot retries database connections at startup because in containerized environments, the database may not be ready when the application starts. This is implemented through Spring Boot's DataSourceHealthIndicator and specifically through the @ConditionalOnSingleCandidate DataSource auto-configuration. The actual retry behavior depends on the connection pool: HikariCP by default throws immediately if no connection can be obtained within connectionTimeout (30 seconds). To enable retry, configure: spring.datasource.hikari.initialization-fail-timeout=-1 (negative value means retry indefinitely). Alternatively, Spring Boot 2.x introduced spring.datasource.url with connection retry support. A better approach for Kubernetes is to use an init container that blocks until the database is ready, or use Spring Retry on the DataSource initialization. In Spring Boot 3.x, you can configure the health check retry with management.health.db.enabled. For production, always separate readiness probes from liveness probes: readiness probe checks DB connectivity, liveness only checks if the JVM is running. This way a DB outage marks the pod as not-ready (stops traffic) without killing and restarting it.

question: How do you manage feature toggles safely?
answer: Feature toggles (feature flags) allow deploying code without activating it, enabling gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant rollbacks. Safe management requires: (1) Centralized toggle store — store flags in a database, Redis, or a dedicated service like LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or Flagsmith. This allows changing flags without redeployment. (2) Type-safe toggle definitions — define toggles as strongly-typed classes, not magic strings. (3) Default-off principle — new features default to disabled. Never default a breaking change to enabled. (4) Toggle lifecycle — toggles have owners and expiry dates. Old toggles must be removed from code after full rollout to prevent accumulation of dead code paths. (5) Gradual rollout — enable for 5% of users, then 20%, then 100%. Route decisions by user ID hash. (6) Circuit breaker integration — if a new code path under a toggle causes errors, automatically disable the toggle. In Spring Boot, ff4j and Togglz are popular libraries. With Spring Cloud Config, you can change properties at runtime and expose them as feature flags.

question: Why does @Cacheable sometimes not cache?
answer: @Cacheable fails silently in several scenarios. (1) Self-invocation — the most common cause. If a method annotated with @Cacheable is called from within the same class (this.method()), Spring's AOP proxy is bypassed. The method runs but the result is not cached. Fix: inject the bean into itself or use ApplicationContext.getBean(). (2) Method is not public — Spring AOP only proxies public methods by default. @Cacheable on private or package-private methods is silently ignored. (3) Exception thrown — if the method throws, the result is not cached. This is correct behavior. (4) Condition or unless not evaluated as expected — @Cacheable(condition = "#result != null") uses SpEL and unless prevents caching null results. Mistakes in SpEL expressions silently bypass caching. (5) Cache manager not configured — if no CacheManager bean exists, Spring Boot auto-configures a no-op cache manager in some scenarios. Verify /actuator/caches shows your cache. (6) Key collision — all calls share the same cache key if you don't specify a key. By default the key is all parameters combined. (7) The cache type (e.g., Redis) has a serialization issue — the object is not serializable, so caching silently fails.

question: How does Spring Boot isolate environment-specific configs?
answer: Spring Boot uses profiles as the primary mechanism for environment-specific configuration. The profile system works at multiple levels: (1) Profile-specific property files — application-dev.yml, application-prod.yml, application-test.yml. Values in profile-specific files override base application.yml values. Activate with spring.profiles.active=prod or SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod environment variable. (2) @Profile annotation on @Bean methods or @Configuration classes — a bean only exists in the specified profile. (3) @ConfigurationProperties with profile-specific values — the same class is populated with different values per profile. (4) Spring Cloud Config Server — externalized configuration in a Git repo or Vault, with profile-specific config files per service. Supports hot reload via /actuator/refresh. (5) Environment variables override — in production, sensitive values (DB passwords, API keys) should come from environment variables, not config files in VCS. Spring Boot automatically maps MY_DB_PASSWORD to my.db.password. (6) Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets mounted as environment variables or files — Spring Boot reads them transparently. Best practice: config files in VCS contain non-sensitive defaults; environments override via environment variables or Config Server.

question: What causes classloader issues in fat JARs?
answer: Spring Boot packages applications as fat JARs (executable JARs with all dependencies nested inside). This creates a custom classloader (LaunchedURLClassLoader) that loads classes from nested JARs — a non-standard mechanism the JVM doesn't support natively. This causes several issues: (1) Class.forName() failures — if code uses reflection to load classes by name, the class must be on the classloader's path. In fat JARs, the classpath structure is different from exploded directories. (2) ServiceLoader failures — META-INF/services files must be on the root classpath. Multiple JARs with the same service file can cause only one to be visible. The spring-boot-maven-plugin merges these correctly, but other fat JAR plugins may not. (3) SLF4J 'multiple bindings' — if two logging implementations (logback and log4j-slf4j) are on the classpath, only one is used but both are loaded. (4) Conflicting library versions — when both version A and version B of a library are needed by different dependencies, the classloader loads whichever is found first. Use spring-boot-dependencies BOM to align versions. (5) Native libraries (.so, .dll) — cannot be loaded from nested JARs; must be extracted. (6) Thread context classloader — when spawning threads that load classes, ensure they inherit the correct classloader.

question: How do you safely reload configs without restarting?
answer: Config hot-reload in Spring Boot is possible through Spring Cloud Config's refresh mechanism. The flow: (1) Spring Cloud Config Server serves configuration from Git or Vault. (2) Client application uses spring-cloud-starter-config and exposes @RefreshScope beans. (3) When config changes, a POST to /actuator/refresh triggers: re-fetching properties from Config Server, re-binding @ConfigurationProperties beans, re-initializing @RefreshScope beans. (4) For automatic triggering, use Spring Cloud Bus with Kafka or RabbitMQ — a webhook from your Git provider posts to one instance which broadcasts the refresh event to all instances via the message broker. Critical caveat: @RefreshScope recreates the bean on next access after refresh. If the bean holds expensive state (connections, caches), recreation is costly. Beans NOT in @RefreshScope keep their original property values even after refresh. Database connection pool properties (hikari) are NOT refreshable — they require restart. Only application-level properties are safely refreshable. For Kubernetes, ConfigMap changes can be propagated without restart using Spring Cloud Kubernetes Config Watcher.

question: Why does logging behave differently in prod vs local?
answer: Logging discrepancies between prod and local arise from several sources. (1) Log level differences — local typically runs at DEBUG or INFO while prod runs at WARN or ERROR to reduce I/O. A bug that only manifests with TRACE-level Hibernate SQL logging won't be seen in prod. (2) Async vs synchronous logging — prod environments use async appenders (AsyncAppender, log4j2 async) for performance. Async logging can lose the last few log messages if the JVM crashes before the queue is flushed. (3) Log format differences — local uses human-readable text; prod uses JSON (for ELK/Splunk ingestion). JSON logging can cause issues if log messages contain unescaped special characters. (4) Log rotation and disk space — prod logs rotate at 100MB; if a log storm occurs it fills disk, which can then cause the logging framework to block the application threads. (5) MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) — tracing headers (X-Request-ID, trace IDs) are added to MDC in filters. If the filter ordering differs between environments, MDC may be empty. (6) Logback vs Log4j2 — the two have different async semantics and configuration mechanisms. Ensure the same library is used in both environments.
codeExample:
// logback-spring.xml — profile-specific config
<configuration>
    <springProfile name="local">
        <appender name="CONSOLE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
            <encoder>
                <pattern>%d{HH:mm:ss} %-5level %logger{36} - %msg%n</pattern>
            </encoder>
        </appender>
        <root level="DEBUG"><appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/></root>
    </springProfile>

    <springProfile name="prod">
        <appender name="FILE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.RollingFileAppender">
            <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder"/>
            <rollingPolicy class="...TimeBasedRollingPolicy">
                <fileNamePattern>app-%d{yyyy-MM-dd}.log</fileNamePattern>
                <maxHistory>30</maxHistory>
                <totalSizeCap>10GB</totalSizeCap>
            </rollingPolicy>
        </appender>
        <appender name="ASYNC" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.AsyncAppender">
            <discardingThreshold>0</discardingThreshold>
            <appender-ref ref="FILE"/>
        </appender>
        <root level="INFO"><appender-ref ref="ASYNC"/></root>
    </springProfile>
</configuration>

// MDC tracing filter
@Component
public class TraceFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    @Override protected void doFilterInternal(
            HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res, FilterChain chain)
            throws ServletException, IOException {
        MDC.put("traceId", UUID.randomUUID().toString());
        try { chain.doFilter(req, res); }
        finally { MDC.clear(); }
    }
}

question: How do you handle partial failures in dependent services?
answer: Partial failures — where a downstream service is slow, returning errors for some requests, or completely unreachable — must be handled explicitly. Unhandled, they cause cascading failures where the caller's thread pool exhausts waiting for a slow downstream, making the caller also unavailable. The Resilience4j library is the standard solution in Spring Boot. Key patterns: (1) Circuit Breaker — after a threshold of failures, opens the circuit and fails fast for a configured duration without calling the downstream. Closed -> Open -> Half-Open -> Closed cycle. (2) Retry — automatically retry failed calls with exponential backoff and jitter. Only retry on transient errors (5xx, timeout), not on 4xx. (3) Timeout — every external call must have a deadline. Never use the default which is often infinite. (4) Bulkhead — limit concurrent calls to a downstream to prevent one slow service from consuming all threads. ThreadPoolBulkhead uses a separate thread pool; SemaphoreBulkhead limits concurrent executions. (5) Fallback — return a degraded response (cached data, empty list, default value) when the downstream is unavailable. The system remains functional, just with reduced capability.

question: What is the real impact of using too many interceptors?
answer: Spring MVC interceptors (HandlerInterceptor) run preHandle, postHandle, and afterCompletion for every request that matches their URL pattern. Excessive interceptors cause: (1) Cumulative latency — each interceptor adds overhead. If 10 interceptors each take 2ms, that's 20ms added to every request. At 10,000 RPS this becomes significant. (2) Thread blocking — interceptors run synchronously on request threads. Any I/O in an interceptor (logging to database, calling a remote service for auth token validation) blocks the thread. (3) Memory overhead — interceptors that add data to the request scope (MDC entries, model attributes) consume memory proportional to request rate. (4) Ordering complexity — interceptors execute in registration order (preHandle), and in reverse order (postHandle, afterCompletion). Incorrect ordering causes subtle bugs where, for example, auth interceptor runs after logging interceptor and logs unauthenticated request details. (5) Exception masking — an exception in postHandle or afterCompletion can mask the original controller exception. Best practices: keep interceptors stateless and allocation-free. Move heavy cross-cutting concerns to AOP aspects or filters. Use specific URL patterns rather than /**. Avoid calling external services in interceptors — use the Async context or queue the work.

question: How do you prevent breaking changes during deployments?
answer: Zero-downtime deployments with multiple service versions running simultaneously require careful API and database change management. API-level strategies: (1) Semantic versioning in URL paths (/api/v1/, /api/v2/) — old clients use v1, new clients use v2. Run both versions simultaneously during rollout. (2) Backward-compatible changes only — add optional fields, never remove or rename required fields. Use @JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown=true) to accept unknown fields from newer clients. (3) API deprecation headers — return Deprecation: true and Sunset: <date> HTTP headers on old endpoints to signal clients to migrate. Database-level strategies (expand-contract pattern): (1) Expand phase — add new columns as nullable, add new tables. Both old and new code work. (2) Migrate phase — backfill data, update application code to use new structure. (3) Contract phase — remove old columns/tables after all instances use new code. Never drop a column in the same deployment that removes code using it. For Kafka: maintain backward-compatible schema using Schema Registry with Avro/Protobuf. Never change field types or remove required fields. Add new fields as optional with defaults.

question: Why does @ConfigurationProperties fail silently?
answer: @ConfigurationProperties binding fails silently in several scenarios. (1) Missing @EnableConfigurationProperties or @ConfigurationPropertiesScan — the class must be registered as a Spring bean. Without this, the class exists but is never populated from properties. In Spring Boot 2.2+, adding @ConfigurationProperties to a class annotated with @Component works, but the preferred approach is @ConfigurationPropertiesScan on the main class. (2) Constructor binding without @ConstructorBinding — if using immutable records or classes with final fields, Spring Boot 2.2+ requires explicit @ConstructorBinding (removed in 3.x, handled automatically with records). (3) Type mismatch — if a property value cannot be converted to the target type (e.g., 'abc' into int), binding silently uses the default value in non-strict mode or fails at startup if @Validated is used. (4) Prefix mismatch — a typo in the @ConfigurationProperties(prefix = 'payment') vs the actual property key 'payments.timeout' causes silent non-binding. (5) Relaxed binding works in one direction — 'myApp.apiKey', 'my-app.api-key', 'MY_APP_API_KEY' all bind to myApp.apiKey. But if the field name doesn't match any relaxed variant of the property key, it stays null without an error. Always add @Validated with constraint annotations to catch these silently-missed bindings.

question: What Spring Boot decision caused you a real production issue?
answer: A common real production issue: using spring.jpa.open-in-view=true (the default). This setting opens a Hibernate Session for the entire duration of an HTTP request, including the view rendering phase — long after the controller method has returned. The consequence: (1) DB connections are held for the full duration of view rendering (template processing, JSON serialization), not just the transaction. (2) Under high load, HikariCP pool exhausts. New requests wait for connections that are held by requests that have already completed their DB work but are still serializing JSON. (3) Symptoms: intermittent 503 errors, hikari.connections.pending > 0, response times spike under moderate load. This is a silent anti-pattern because the application works fine at low traffic. Discovery: enabling /actuator/metrics/hikari.connections.pending revealed the pool was saturating. The fix was setting spring.jpa.open-in-view=false and ensuring all lazy associations were eagerly fetched within @Transactional boundaries. This required auditing every service method that returned entities and either adding JOIN FETCH queries or converting to DTOs inside the transaction. Spring Boot logs a WARN about this setting at startup but most developers dismiss it.
